Looks good at first glance, I haven't tested the code, though. From reviewing the code, I do have some remarks:
 - You say the stty stuff is no longer needed, but are you sure? AFAICS that code was intended to check whether the Linux side of the connection supported a particular baudrate and then switch the PN532 to the highest baudrate supported by the particular serial port. I think that Linux serial port baudrate support has been spotty in the past (different drivers implemented different APIs and different selections of baudrates), I'm not sure how this is now.
 - With the stty code gone, you now always switch to the hardcoded `max_baudrate`, there is no longer any chance that another baudrate is switched to. Given that, it doesn't make much sense to probe the device with all baudrates (between default and max ) - just the default and max would be sufficient (assuming that you are only trying to recover from a situation where nfcpy was previously used to talk to the device, but if you want to support the case where another process (or a previous nfcpy with a different max_baudrate set) has configured the device, then it makes sense to probe all baudrates, but then I'd think you need really probe all, including the ones below default_baudrate).
 - This relates to #188 but does not really solve all sides of that issue: To disable baudrate changes, I now still need to change the nfcpy sourcecode, it would be nice if there was a way to set the max_baudrate through the connection-making API (but that might be out of scope for this PR, of course).
Some works needs to be done on the tests and documentation, but I think this approach is shaping up well

I think we're making some assumptions in this diff:
- GetLogdirSubdirectories is always called (e.g. by plugin_event_accumulator) before ListDirectoryAbsolute is called on the lower level dir (e.g. by directory_watcher). Because the cache build by the former will be used by that latter
- GetLogdirSubdirectories is called on the highest top level dir once, because we'll clear cache every time
- Our implementation of listdir_bfs will be just as or more efficient than gfile.walk, since we're now implementing walk explicitly instead of relying on the walk implementation of each filesystem
- Without a TTL for the cache, we're no longer supporting watching of new files as they're created after TB initial load

I think we need much fewer change, if we simply build a look aside cache with TTL inside ListDirectoryAbsolute, without touching GetLogdirSubdirectories?

Would love to hear thoughts from TB team.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [x] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [x] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

Hi @jamesrusso, first of all I think this is a really good approach. I tested this and it works if the buffer length changes as you stated. However, the original problem persists if the buffer length stays the same.

This gave me an idea. As a test, I took a similar approach and saved a reference to the buffer and used as "is not" comparison which seems to fix the issue even if the buffer length stays the same. Anyway, here's the code segment I got to work if you'd like to give it a try and maybe apply it to your PR:
```
# a 'static' object that we will use to manage our PWM DMA channel
# we only support one LED strip per raspi
_led_strip = None
_buf = None

def neopixel_write(gpio, buf):
    """NeoPixel Writing Function"""
    global _led_strip  # we'll have one strip we init if its not at first
    global _buf # we save a reference to the buf, and if it changes we will cleanup and re-initalize.

    if _led_strip is None or buf is not _buf:
        # This is safe to call since it doesn't do anything if _led_strip is None
        neopixel_cleanup()

        # Create a ws2811_t structure from the LED configuration.
        # Note that this structure will be created on the heap so you
        # need to be careful that you delete its memory by calling
        # delete_ws2811_t when it's not needed.
        _led_strip = ws.new_ws2811_t()
        _buf = buf
```

This one is a big deal. Without this change `p` was getting deleted twice - once in `pybind11_meta_dealloc` and once on shutdown in "regular" GC. It could be a CPython bug, or it could be pybind11. Things that silence the error:

app = Starlette(

Due to problem of filter restrictions, I can't just rely on the response count, as there's some cases where multiple networks of the same name will be returned in the API response, but only 1 of those will be a valid network. I see that you've put a lot of work into this and appreciate it, and to that end I've tried to give a more thorough review of the potential issues I see in this implementation.

However, I do have some structural concerns that give me pause about merging this:
* There are still various cases that may or may not be handled properly in all scenarios, in particular, at the very least I some issues I see with unions, custom subclasses of `list`, and custom subclasses of `AbstractSet`.
* This essentially aims to duplicate one conceptual part of the logic of model validation, but doesn't follow the same code paths or anything, and is likely to not exactly match the existing behavior in various cases (including the ones called out in the previous point). And without duplicating a huge swath of tests, it seems to me highly likely that we will eventually introduce a change in the validation logic (e.g., more config settings) that won't be properly respected by the recursive construct function.
* I'll also note that adding these `if` branches into the `model_construct` method will have performance implications (even if small) for non-users of the recursion.

Given all the potential issues here, I suspect that a _better_ way to implement this would be to create a `construct` function in pydantic-core which differs from validation in precisely the ways you want — basically, handling everything besides a `BaseModel` subclass as `Any`, with special treatment for generic containers that contain `BaseModel` subclasses at some level down in their parameters.

I think that would be much more performant, and also much easier to keep in sync with the behavior of the non-model-construct logic in the library, and if implemented well, easier to describe the behavior.

If we continue down the path of this implementation, I suspect there will be a lot of subtle inconsistencies/misbehaviors with this feature, and it will be a source of issue reports we need to maintain for some time; as a result, I think the other maintainers are likely to be averse to accepting a feature like this, but I'll let them speak for themselves — @samuelcolvin , @adriangb, anyone else who sees this..

If the other maintainers agree with me that this seems like it would be a _significant_ maintenance burden to add, I think an approach that might be more likely to get this integrated into the library in the long term would be to create a separate package on PyPI called something like `pydantic-recursive-construct` that implements recursive model construct as a standalone function, and get some adoption and work out any kinks. Then re-open a PR on pydantic once the implementation is a bit more battle tested. But again, I'm not sure what the other maintainers might have to say.

@YosuaMichael I'm not sure we need this patch. Before we extract anything, we make sure the checksum of the downloaded file matches the one we have on record:

I'm still concerned about the fact this applies to both deserialization and serialization.

As I wrote in [this comment](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/1549#issuecomment-809123977), I generally see the field name as expressing the deserialized type, while parameters can be used to tweak serialization.

```py
class FooField(Field):  # Will deserialiaze as Foo instance.
    __init__(self,..., serialize_as="baz"):  # Will serialize as baz
        ...
```

Thanks for the PR @oke-aditya! About your questions:

1. IMO this basically reduces to: do we allow batch processing or not. Given that we cant parallelize this due to our `PIL` usage I'd say we don't allow it. Passing batches would internally mean we use a for loop anyway and thus the user has no real advantage. I think we can simply `try` to `squeeze(0)` if we encounter an image with 4 dimensions and handle the error if this fails.
2. Yes, we should return the tensor. Why wouldn't we or more importantly: why would a user use this function if he gets no results back?
3. It can only be transparent if we use a forth channel for the alpha, i.e. change the image from `RGB` to `RGBA`. I'm not sure if this is a good idea. Is it common to fill the bounding boxes? I've never seen it before, which does not mean it does not exist. Otherwise I see no point in adding a `fill` option.
4. I agree testing this will not be easy, but I think we can cover some basic stuff. For example you can start with an all white image, draw a bounding box and check if only the pixels you would expect are changed. You can also test the colors with this. 

In addition to your questions, I have some other remarks below. The linter is also failing, but lets postpone this until we have the functionality right.

@vaimdev  Noticed a few things --

- `print(...)` => remove or `logger.debug(...)`
- Exceptions should be clear on the immediate issue, and for non-expert users, recommended work around
- Exceptions around server issues should handle the case of backwards compatibility (new pygraphistry, old nexus; old pygraphistry, new nexus) as this does not merit a breaking change, afaict. For example, you can test against the current `hub.graphistry.com`, which might not take org id as a param
- More on the server side vs this PR: we should do an ACL check that the user has editor access to the intended org (no forgery/masquerading...)
- There are two upload flows that happen, `.plot(as_files=True)` vs `.plot(as_files=False)` (default)
  - the `as_files=False` case (default) will create a `Dataset` with metadata, at which point we should put in the org id. The  actual individual node/edge buffer uploads should not need to re-specify it (`post_arrow`). I'm not even sure  the backend supports changing the POST args.
  - the `as_files=True` case (long-term preferred for incrementalization opts) will create a `File` (edges), upload an arrow, another `File` (nodes), upload another arrow, and then create a new `Dataset` that refers to the two `file_id`'s. Creating the `Dataset` + `File`s should send the org id metadata; the actual buffer upload need not re-specify the org (and again, changing the calling convention might break it...)

I see your improvement from 41s to 6s is pretty significant so its definitly worth continuing this train of thought. I'm just trying to prevent tons of parameters being added to methods... > If you would be in dire need for a speedup quickly, probably a quick fix would be to increase the default batch size to 100 🤔? although I like it to be configurable
> 
> @adehad @ssbarnea some architectural guidance is welcome :-)

@rynkk, thanks for this. Especially as this is one of the more convoluted areas in the code.
As suggested by @studioj I believe this batch size needs to be set at a class instance level. i.e. in here something like DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE
https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/678903805e34d91c3905d73d20fcc7a3f5c8036c/jira/client.py#L343

I completely understand where you are coming from with wanting to set this per function. It provides the most flexibility, especially as you will likely have more issues than dashboards and therefore would want to set it differently for different resources. Also scales differently depending on how fast the server is you are working with.

A solution I would prefer if you strongly would like to set this per endpoint might look like so:

```python
# within the DEFAULT_OPTIONS

# A dict of (Resource Type, Batch Size) 
DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE: Dict[Resource, int] = {
    Issue : 500,
    Resource: 100,
}


...

# inside the JIRA.__init__

if not default_batch_sizes.get(Resource):
    _LOG.warning() # warn that the user will be defaulting to our default
    default_batch_sizes[Resource] = 50 # or some arbritrary
self.options["default_batch_size"] = default_batch_sizes  # or whatever the argument name is



...
# now inside fetch pages

        if maxResults:
            page_params["maxResults"] = maxResults
        else: # will need to set batch size to get all resources
            page_params["maxResults"] = self.options["default_batch_size"].get(item_type, self.options["default_batch_size"].get(Resource))
            _LOG.verbose(f"Batch size for {item_type.__name__} set to {page_params["maxResults"]}")
```

Hi @Joshix-1,

this seems to be about invocation `ansi2html --partial`.  I'm not a big fan of this pull request as-is because:
- Use of `<style>` is not allowed outside of `<head>` according to https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/style .
- Current users of `--partial` would experience backwards-incompatible behavior.
- Working around `--inline` is likely not worth the savings in 99% of environments today, besides purism. I'd be curious how you environment is different.
- (The CI is failing.)
- (There are no new related tests yet.)
- (There are seemingly unrelated changes to white space of the generated code in the PR.)

If this direction is of true importance, maybe adding a new mode argument `--partial-plus-style` would be more clear and backwards-compatible.  My personal vote would be to re-evaluate `--inline` first, though. What do you think?

Best, Sebastian

Clarifying the design here:
```
class RLOptimizer:

	def configure_optimizers():
		pass

	def compute_loss():
		pass

	def on_before_compute_loss():
		pass

	def on_after_compute_loss():
		pass

###### NOTE: this is not in the docstring format, please modify it to a more concise form for the purpose of the docstring.
 Please also don't read too much in the name of attributes and methods that I picked here. We can finalize the names once 
 the functionality is fully fleshed out. 
 

###### also note that all this code is getting run and constructed on a single process

class RLTrainerActor:

	def __init__():
		# the single/multi agent Module that is constructed inside the RLTrainer
		self.module = ...
		# the module is passed by reference to the optimizer
		self.rl_optimizer = RLOptimizer(self.module, ...)
		# an alias to the optimizers object created after we invoke this method
		self.optimizers = self.rl_optimizer.configure_optimizers()

	def update(fwd_in, **kwargs):
		fwd_in: SampleBatchType = ...

		# if module is MARLModule sample will be MABatch and the output will also be in MA format
		fwd_out = self.module.forward_train(fwd_in=sample)
		###### ----> This is where the on_before_compute_loss() could go
		loss_out = self.rl_optimizer.compute_loss(fwd_in, fwd_out)
		###### ----> This is where on_after_compute_loss() could go and so on. 
		self.rl_optimizer.log_and_compile(fwd_in, fwd_out, loss_out)
		grads = self.compute_gradients(loss_out)
		self.apply_gradients(grads)

	def compute_gradients(loss_out):
		# for torch
		loss_out["loss_total"].backward()
		grads = {n: p.grad for n, p in self.module.named_parameters()}
		return grads

	def apply_gradients(grads):
		# for torch
		self.optimizers.step()

# the RLTrainerActor will itself be managed by the TrainerRunner which will coordinate the RLTrainerActors and which 
module they will be responsible for. The important point is that  RLTrainerActor's code does not care whether the underlying 
module is a MARLModule or RLModule or whether the rl_optimizer is RLOptimizer or MARLOptimizer. The API and 
functionality looks the  same. 


```

Based on the findings, I would just change the commit message / PR description to something like this then:

```
Fix for #89, List Items separated by tab character not parsed correctly.

Also fixes failing examples 312 and 313 in the CommonMark 0.30 spec due to the way leading space is now checked for list items.

The 1st problem was that `# check if next_line starts List` considered only markers followed
by a space and not by a tab. Another problem was that the implemented tab expansion
to tab stops (of width 4 as per spec) could work only in a limited scope.

This fix uses `expandtabs()` to implement the tab stops correctly and moves extraction
of content into the `parse_marker()` and `parse_continuation()` methods. This lets
us implement use cases like "list interrupts a paragraph" and "list item continuation"
in a less error-prone way.
```

Just my two cents here, but I personally feel this goes along the very principle of using classes.

The point of OOP is you can subclass your view, and implement whatever in your `on_check_failure_func` , if you'd like you can defer. Forcing a non-sane default on the pretence that everyone would prefer it that way is not the best idea. 

Also your argument falls flat, when you talk about ugliness. How is two lines of code in your subclasses on_check_failure is ugly? 

You also fail to address rate limits, as Sarc had mentioned.

My final thought would be that I like the error in the UI, the user gets a clear message that shows them that the interaction failed. Letting the user get a direct and clear signal from the UI itself is superior to having to send messages manually.

tl;dr just subclass and write your own, don't force defaults.
